schools.summary: Gill: Ananias's account follows and is opposed to Barnabas's true generosity; his name ('the grace of the Lord') ironic, for he was graceless — hypocrites among the greatest names and gifts (perhaps one of the 120, gifts without grace); the first recorded hypocrites in the Christian church, the visible church always containing false among true; their sin springing from coveting the reputation of generosity without its cost (vanity and covetousness joined).

themes: Ananias with Sapphira sold a possession

schools.summary: Gill: Ananias reserved part for himself while professing to give all for the church; Lightfoot: 'kept back' (enosphisato) the word used of Achan's embezzling (Joshua 7), a fraudulent withholding; the sin was the lie (pretending full generosity), not the keeping (the money was his own, v.4); 'his wife also being privy' — a joint conspiracy, both guilty, the union perverted to mutual deceit, the desire to seem more devoted than one is.

themes: kept back part of the price

schools.summary: Gill: Peter knew the secret by revelation or a spirit of discerning (as Elisha, 2 Kings 5:26); Lightfoot: the striking contrast of Peter (who had denied with a lie and oath) now judging another's lie, his restoration complete; 'Satan filled thine heart' — the tempter prompting, yet Ananias consenting (the devil's filling no excuse); 'lie to the Holy Ghost' — a sin against the Spirit in the apostles, who is God (v.4), a witness to the Spirit's deity.

debates.question: Does Satan's filling of the heart excuse Ananias?

sides.position: No — Satan prompted, but Ananias consented, his own covetousness opening the door; he is charged with the sin

themes: why hath Satan filled thine heart
to lie to the Holy Ghost

schools.summary: Gill/Barnes: the selling was voluntary, a free act of liberality not enjoined (against compulsory communism), the giving each as he purposed (2 Corinthians 9:7); Ananias's sin not the keeping but the lie; the climactic 'thou hast not lied unto men, but unto God' — the lie to the Holy Ghost (v.3) called a lie to God, identifying the Spirit with God, a clear witness to the Spirit's deity (a divine Person, not a mere influence); the folly of deceiving the omniscient God.

debates.question: What does Acts 5:4 teach about the Holy Spirit?

sides.position: His deity — the lie 'to the Holy Ghost' (v.3) is here 'to God,' identifying the Spirit with God

themes: thou hast not lied unto men, but unto God
the deity of the Holy Spirit

schools.summary: Barnes: the charge came on Ananias like a thunderbolt, 'gave up the ghost' = he died; the death a direct, immediate judgment of God (not Peter's hand nor natural cause), a signal example of hypocrisy punished in the church's infancy to establish its purity and the awe of God; God's hatred of hypocrisy (a sin seeming small but abhorred); 'great fear' a salutary effect, purifying the church and impressing all with God's holiness and justice.

themes: Ananias fell down and gave up the ghost

schools.summary: Gill: 'the young men' the younger, stronger brethren fit for the service, rising at once unwilling the awful spectacle should lie long; Lightfoot: they bound the body in what cloths they could and carried it out (the burying-places outside the city, the Jewish custom to bury without delay); the prompt, decent, orderly burial maintaining decency even in judgment's aftermath, the ~three-hour interval providentially setting the stage for Sapphira's coming.

themes: the young men wound him up
carried him out, and buried him

schools.summary: Barnes: Sapphira no less guilty than her husband (privy to the scheme, v.2), so God's providence ordered the same judgment on both — the impartiality of divine justice, partnership in sin bringing partnership in punishment; Gill/Lightfoot account for the three hours by the distance to the burial-place; her coming 'not knowing' providentially set the stage for her testing, giving her (like Ananias) the chance to confess or persist in the lie.

themes: about the space of three hours after
equal justice on the equally guilty

schools.summary: Barnes: the falsehood — it was true they received that sum, but they received more; representing the part as the whole to deceive is as real a falsehood as claiming more, falsehood being any erroneous representation made to deceive; Peter's question a fair chance to confess (not entrapment), her lie 'Yea, for so much' a deliberate persistence when offered the truth (three hours to reflect), the subtlety of the misleading half-truth, the lost opportunity sealing her guilt.

themes: sold ye the land for so much?

schools.summary: Gill: for husband and wife to agree in good is commendable, but to agree in a fraud and lie is dreadful — the union perverted to a partnership in deceit; Barnes: 'tempt the Spirit' = to try whether the Spirit could detect their crime, a daring presumption against his omniscience; the conspiracy doubling the guilt; 'the Spirit of the Lord' again the God against whom they sinned (v.4); Peter's Spirit-given foreknowledge of Sapphira's death (the same judgment for the same sin), the feet at the door.

themes: agreed together to tempt the Spirit
the feet at the door shall carry thee out
tempting the Spirit of the Lord

schools.summary: Clarke: Sapphira's death by an immediate judgment of God (not Peter's words, prayers, or shame); the equal justice on the equally guilty, buried beside her husband; Clarke weighs their eternal state — 'a sin unto death' (1 John 5:16) punished with temporal death, yet mercy perhaps to the soul; the witnesses hold cautious reserve (some hope the souls saved as by fire, 1 Corinthians 3:15; others fear their doom), not presuming where Scripture is silent.

debates.question: What was the eternal state of Ananias and Sapphira?

sides.position: A 'sin unto death' — temporal death, yet mercy perhaps to the soul (saved as by fire)
Their hypocrisy may mark them unregenerate; the question left undeclared by Scripture

schools.summary: Clarke: the judgment answered its end — a deeply religious fear banishing hypocrisy from the holy assembly; here a specimen of a NT church (called by the gospel, grafted by baptism, animated by love, united in fellowship, disciplined by the punishment of hypocrites); the fear wholesome (holy reverence, the beginning of wisdom, Proverbs 9:10), not slavish terror, love and fear together keeping the church pure and warm, the purity advancing its witness.

themes: great fear came upon all the church
a specimen of a true church

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: many miracles of mercy for the one of judgment — the gospel power returning to its proper channel of mercy and grace, God returning from punishing to his mercy-seat; Gill: the signs wrought by the apostles as God's instruments; judgment God's 'strange work' (Isaiah 28:21), mercy his proper and delighted working; the church gathered with one accord in Solomon's porch, one in heart, the miracles done publicly, both judgment and mercy from the same God.

themes: many signs and wonders by the apostles
with one accord in Solomon's porch
the gospel power returning to grace

schools.summary: Gill weighs who 'the rest' were (the profane who durst not approach, or the insincere fearing to join hypocritically after Ananias); the awe of the judgment and miracles restrained the false from joining lightly (none dared come in hypocritically, lest exposed), keeping the church pure; yet 'the people magnified them' — the church esteemed though not lightly joined, the holiness repelling the false while drawing the true (the sincere added, v.14).

themes: of the rest durst no man join
purity repelling the false, drawing the true

schools.summary: Gill: the case of Ananias and Sapphira, far from hindering, was followed by greater additions than before — of true believers; the growth not despite but through the church's purity (the judgment deterring the false, drawing the sincere); the added 'believers... added to the Lord' (genuine converts, joined to Christ then the church), quality not mere quantity; 'both of men and women' the breadth of the gospel; the holiness the attraction to the sincere.

themes: believers were the more added to the Lord
multitudes both of men and women

schools.summary: Gill: the sick brought into every street to wait for the apostles; Barnes: 'beds' the soft beds of the rich (rich and poor alike laid out), and carefully notes the healing is 'expressly affirmed' of those from the cities (v.16) but not directly of those under Peter's shadow; some take the shadow as healing (God honoring faith, as Christ's garment-hem, Matthew 9:21; Paul's handkerchiefs, Acts 19:12), others (Barnes) note only the people's expectation is stated — the great eager faith certain, God ready to honor it.

debates.question: Did Peter's shadow heal the sick?

sides.position: Likely yes — God honoring the people's faith (as Christ's garment-hem, Paul's handkerchiefs)
The text affirms the people's expectation, not directly the cure by the shadow (Barnes' careful reading)

themes: brought the sick into the streets
the shadow of Peter might overshadow some

schools.summary: Barnes: the multitude attracted by the fame of Peter's miracles (as once by Christ's); the burdens — the sick and those 'vexed with unclean spirits' (demon-possessed), the apostles' power over disease and the powers of darkness; 'healed every one' expressly affirmed (not some but all, certain and complete) — the inspired writer careful to state plainly what was done (vs the people's expectation, v.15); the apostles continuing Christ's works (John 14:12), the signs confirming the gospel and drawing multitudes.

themes: a multitude out of the cities round about
the complete cures attesting the gospel

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the Sadducees roused and excited by the events, assaulting the apostles more vigorously; Robertson: the word 'jealousy' (zēlou) — zeal turned bad, envy; the high priest's party (Sadducees, who denied the resurrection, Acts 23:8) filled with indignation at the apostles' success and the people's esteem; the opposition rooted in jealousy (as 'for envy they delivered Christ,' Matthew 27:18), the resurrection-preaching the special offense to those who denied it.

themes: the high priest and the Sadducees

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'common'/'public' prison perhaps indicates greater severity — the apostles dealt with as common criminals, herding with ruffians and robbers; the opposition escalating (from threats, Acts 4:21, to imprisoning the whole company); Robertson confirms the jealousy driving it; the apostles repaid for their good (healing, preaching life) with imprisonment as felons, the servants treated as their Master, the providence overruling the arrest for deliverance and further testimony.

themes: laid their hands on the apostles
put them in the common prison

schools.summary: Gill: a good, elect angel (a ministering spirit to the heirs of salvation, Hebrews 1:14, excelling in strength); Barnes: evidently a miracle, an angel for this special purpose; Chrysostom: the deliverance both for the disciples' encouragement and the others' instruction; the angel opening the locked, guarded prison by night — the ministry of angels to God's servants, God's power over the enemies' devices (the prison men shut, the angel opened), the deliverance freeing them for further service.

themes: the angel of the Lord by night
God's power over the enemies' devices

schools.summary: Gill: the apostles delivered not to secure themselves but to do their appointed work — to preach in the temple (the public place) and stand with courage, not fearing men's wrath; Lightfoot: 'the words of this life' = the gospel of eternal life; the deliverance for service not self-preservation, the boldness commanded (sent straight back to the danger), the fullness — 'all the words,' the whole counsel of God, the complete gospel of life held back in nothing.

themes: stand and speak in the temple
all the words of this life

schools.summary: Gill: the apostles obeyed the angel's word, entering the temple at daybreak to teach; Barnes: the Sanhedrin and 'all the senate' (the eldership) gathered to try them, sending to the prison; the apostles' obedience prompt, exact, and fearless (returning at once to the public work for which they were imprisoned, trusting the God who delivered them); the dramatic irony — the council solemnly sending for prisoners already delivered and teaching in the temple, the enemies' plans confounded.

themes: entered the temple early and taught

schools.summary: Robertson: 'the officers' (hypēretai, under-rowers) the servants executing the Sanhedrin's orders, the prison 'shut tight' with sentinels at the doors; the officers found the prison empty yet secure, the prisoners gone — bewildered, they could only report; the empty, guarded prison manifest evidence of a deliverance by no human means, the miracle undeniable, the careful security heightening the wonder and confounding the enemies.

themes: found them not in the prison

schools.summary: Barnes: the prison not broken open (no forced escape), the guards at their posts (not released by them), the officers the only means of entry — every natural explanation excluded, the deliverance manifestly God's interposition; Clarke: all as they left it (doors bolted, guards posted) increasing the astonishment; the miracle established by every circumstance (locked doors, posted guards, empty cell), the enemies' precautions overthrown by God's power, the council confounded.

themes: the prison shut with all safety
the keepers standing before the doors

schools.summary: Barnes: 'doubted' = the anxiety of one who has lost his way and cannot escape his difficulty; the rulers perplexed and anxious 'whereunto this would grow' — their authority disregarded, God opposing them by a miracle (the prison deliverance), the apostles' doctrine spreading; the helplessness of those who fight against God (Psalm 2:4), at a loss to explain or resist his work, in confusion while the apostles preach with confidence — the perplexity of the opposers the foil to the gospel's advance.

themes: they doubted whereunto this would grow

schools.summary: Clarke: while the rulers were perplexed, a messenger surprised them — the very men imprisoned the night before were teaching in the temple; Gill supposes the informant an enemy seeking the rulers' favor; the apostles' boldness (not fleeing but boldly resuming their work in the place of danger) astonishing, deepening the council's confusion — the contrast of bold faith and anxious opposition, the providence turning even the hostile report to display God's deliverance and the futility of the enemies' power.

themes: the men whom ye put in prison
standing in the temple and teaching

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: the second seizing, the first deliverance designed to humble the persecutors' pride and check their fury; Gill: the captain brought them without force; the apostles taken gently 'without violence, for they feared the people' (who esteemed them, v.13) lest they be stoned — the enemies restrained by fear, the providence protecting the apostles through the people's esteem and the rulers' fear, God restraining the wrath of men (Psalm 76:10).

Gill: the apostles brought from the temple to the Sanhedrin's place of session; Robertson: 'they set them' (transitive) before the council; the renewed confrontation — the apostles, delivered and re-arrested, standing again before the very council that imprisoned them, undaunted; the boldness of those who fear God more than men, the high priest preparing to charge them (v.28), the arraignment the occasion for the great confession (v.29) and the advancement of the testimony.

Barnes: 'ye have filled Jerusalem with your doctrine' an unwitting honorable tribute to the apostles' zeal and success (the enemies witnessing the gospel's triumph, a charge the faithful gladly bear, 1 Peter 4:16); the charge of disobeying the command (Acts 4:18); 'bring this man's blood upon us' — the rulers' guilty fear of being charged with Christ's death (who had cried 'his blood be on us,' Matthew 27:25), their conscience betrayed; 'this man' — refusing even to name Jesus, a token of hatred and guilt.

themes: did we not straitly command you
ye have filled Jerusalem with your doctrine
the guilty fear of Christ's blood

schools.summary: Ellicott: the whole Twelve under attack, all accepting Peter as spokesman; Clarke: the same answer as Acts 4:19 — their commission from God, not to be laid down at men's command; the great principle 'we ought to obey God rather than men' — when man's command conflicts with God's, God must be obeyed (authority obeyed in its sphere, Romans 13:1, but not against God's clear command); the charter of conscience against tyranny, stated with reverence not arrogance.

themes: we ought to obey God rather than men

schools.summary: Gill takes 'raised up Jesus' not of the resurrection but of God raising him to the office of Saviour (calling, sending, exhibiting him, per the covenant of grace, as 'raised up a prophet,' Acts 3:22); Barnes takes it of the resurrection; both true (God sent and raised Christ); the apostles grounding in 'the God of our fathers' (no strange God); the contrast — God raised him, 'ye slew' him; 'hanged on a tree' recalling the curse (Deuteronomy 21:23) Christ bore for us (Galatians 3:13), the bold charge to the murderers' faces.

debates.question: Does 'raised up Jesus' mean the resurrection or God's raising him to the office of Saviour?

sides.position: God's raising him up to the office of Saviour (sending/exhibiting him, as Acts 3:22)

themes: the God of our fathers raised up Jesus
whom ye slew and hanged on a tree
the divine exaltation against the human crime
the curse borne on the tree

schools.summary: Gill: 'exalted with his right hand' = by God's power, raised and exalted to the right hand, crowned with glory, all power his; Barnes: 'a Prince' (archēgon, as Acts 3:15) denotes royal dominion; Christ exalted as Prince (Ruler) and Saviour (Redeemer), giving 'repentance to Israel, and forgiveness of sins' — both gifts of the exalted Christ (repentance his gift, 2 Timothy 2:25), bestowed even on Israel who slew him, the riches of grace, the royal and saving offices joined.

themes: God hath exalted him to be a Prince and a Saviour
repentance the gift of the exalted Christ

schools.summary: Gill: the apostles witnessed the incarnation, crucifixion, resurrection, exaltation, offices of Christ, and the influences of grace; Barnes: appointed witnesses (Acts 1:8), the Holy Ghost also witnessing by his descent and the miracles; the double witness (apostles + Spirit) confirming the gospel (Deuteronomy 19:15), the human testimony sealed by the divine; 'the Holy Ghost, whom God hath given to them that obey him' — the Spirit given to the obedient (the obedience of faith), an implicit rebuke to the disobedient rulers who lacked his witness.

themes: so is also the Holy Ghost
the Spirit given to the obedient

schools.summary: Barnes: 'cut to the heart' = to be sawn through, the mind torn with rage and indignation (as a saw tears wood, cf. Acts 7:54); the rulers, hearing the bold charge (they slew Christ whom God exalted) and the Spirit's witness, filled with fury and 'took counsel to slay them'; the contrast with Pentecost ('pricked... unto repentance,' Acts 2:37) — the same truth softening the penitent, hardening the impenitent unto rage; the desperate wickedness answering conviction with murder, restrained by providence (Gamaliel).

Chrysostom: Gamaliel was Paul's teacher (Acts 22:3), so right-minded and learned (Chrysostom hoping he did not remain in unbelief), prudent — addressing the council as if of their opinion, not with vehemence, to cool their passion; a respected Pharisee and doctor of the law; the providence raising up a moderate counselor within the hostile council to restrain its murderous rage (v.33); his stand cautious neutrality ('wait and see'), not saving faith — the difference between political prudence and confession of Christ.

themes: Gamaliel, a doctor of the law
had in reputation among all the people

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Gamaliel at the outset puts the council in fear, counseling men 'intoxicated through passion' not to go to work rashly; Clarke: 'what ye intend to do' = what they were about to do (already resolved to destroy them, v.33); Gamaliel's caution ('take heed to yourselves') checking them at the edge of the murderous act, appealing to their own interest to cool their passion — the providence restraining the rage through a wise counselor, prudent deliberation (short of justice) against rash haste.

themes: ye men of Israel, take heed

schools.summary: Gill: Josephus mentions a Theudas (a prophet-pretender slain by Cuspius Fadus) but later than Gamaliel's speech, a chronological difficulty; the likely harmonization — an earlier Theudas (the name common, such risings frequent in turbulent times) distinct from Josephus's, or Josephus's imprecision; no real contradiction; Gamaliel's principle — merely human movements collapse at their leader's death (Theudas slain, his followers scattered), the argument to be applied to the apostles (v.38).

debates.question: How is Gamaliel's Theudas reconciled with Josephus's later Theudas?

sides.position: An earlier, different Theudas (the name common, such pretenders frequent), or Josephus's imprecision — no real contradiction

themes: before these days rose up Theudas

schools.summary: Barnes: Josephus records Judas of Galilee (a Galilean of Gamala), whose revolt at the census (under Cyrenius) drew much people but perished, his followers dispersed; Lightfoot on the name (Judas the Gaulanite, called 'of Galilee'); the second well-known example confirming the principle — human movements perish with their leader; the cumulative force building to Gamaliel's conclusion (vv.38-39), the contrast (unseen by the council) that the gospel, being of God, did not fail.

themes: Judas of Galilee in the days of the taxing

schools.summary: Barnes: Gamaliel's advice 'refrain... let them alone' — cease opposing, for if the movement is human imposture it will come to nought (as the examples showed), the council needing no trouble; but carefully notes this is uninspired counsel, the principle containing truth but not an absolute rule (some errors endure, some good causes are crushed for a time); the general truth (human works fail) prudent and wise, but not an infallible test of every movement at every moment.

debates.question: Is Gamaliel's principle ('if of men, it will fail') an infallible rule?

sides.position: No — uninspired counsel, generally true but not absolute (error sometimes endures, truth sometimes long oppressed)

themes: refrain from these men, and let them alone
if this work be of men, it will come to nought
the general but not infallible principle

schools.summary: Gill: if the movement is according to God's will, a work to which he called them; Barnes: Gamaliel candidly supposed it at least possible the religion was divine (the resurrection, Pentecost, the healing, the deliverance staggering the council), 'ye cannot overthrow it' because God has almighty power; the invincibility of God's work (Romans 8:31), the solemn warning that to oppose it is to 'fight against God'; yet Gamaliel's cautious neutrality short of saving faith — seeing the danger but not confessing the truth.

themes: if it be of God, ye cannot overthrow it
lest ye be found to fight against God

schools.summary: Barnes: the council agreed with Gamaliel only so far as abandoning the design to kill, not following his advice to let them alone — they beat them (the usual thirty-nine lashes, 2 Corinthians 11:24), commanded silence, and released them; the scourging fulfilling Christ's word (Matthew 10:17, the disciples treated as their Master); the inconsistent half-measure (sparing lives yet beating, heeding Gamaliel and their own malice), the providence bounding the persecution (afflicted but preserved).

themes: commanded silence and let them go

schools.summary: Barnes: a public whipping esteemed deeply disgraceful, the rulers hoping the shame would deter the apostles, but the effect was the reverse — they rejoiced, 'counted worthy to suffer shame for his name'; their joy in the privilege of imitating the Lord Jesus (scourged and reviled), the fellowship of his sufferings (Philippians 3:10; 1 Peter 4:14); the gospel transforming the believer's view — what the world counts shame, the saint counts honor, persecution a privilege not a disgrace.

themes: rejoicing that they were counted worthy
to suffer shame for his name

schools.summary: Gill: the apostles taught daily with constancy and assiduity, publicly (temple) and privately (every house); Clarke: the daily temple-teaching at the hours of prayer commends public worship (every Christian to copy it); undeterred by the beating and the command of silence (v.40), they obeyed God rather than men (v.29), preaching the more (in season and out, 2 Timothy 4:2); 'Jesus Christ' the substance of their preaching — the chapter closing with the gospel undeterred, the church purified, bold, and growing.

themes: daily in the temple and in every house
ceased not to teach and preach Jesus Christ
Christ the substance of their message
